acoustic_nodes.description: Largest PNW owl. Deep resonant hoo-hoo-hoo territorial call. 200-700 Hz fundamental with harmonics. Lowest-pitched owl call in PNW. Physical vibration at close range in dense forest. December-January = most vocally active (earliest PNW breeding species). Male/female duet: male lower, female higher, alternating. Call carries several km in still winter air.

'Who cooks for you' -- rhythmically distinctive 8-note sequence (4+4), ~1.5-2 second intervals. 300-900 Hz. Also produces caterwauling (complex social/territorial vocalization). Eastern NA origin, expanded PNW 20th century. More vocal and territorially aggressive than Spotted Owl. Calls at all hours. Competitive pressure on Northern Spotted.

Four-note sequence: two short notes + pause + one short + longer closing. 400-900 Hz. Similar frequency to Barred Owl, distinct call pattern. ESA threatened 1990. Old-growth forest specialist. Winter vocalizations primarily territorial in response to Barred Owl intrusion. Presence = old-growth forest quality indicator.

Descending tremolo 'bouncing ball' -- series of whistled notes descending in pitch, increasing in rate toward end. 700-1,500 Hz. Highest-pitched primary call in winter owl chorus. Low perches (within 20 feet of ground), shrubby or mixed forest understory. Year-round resident, active throughout winter. Active at dusk and pre-dawn.

theory_nodes.concept: Temporal development model: the mixed owl chorus develops through four phases across the full winter night -- (1) Dusk: Great Horned and Screech-Owl initial territorial establishment; (2) Early night: Barred Owl full calling, Spotted Owl response; (3) Deep night: diminished activity, established territories; (4) Pre-dawn: activity resurgence before dawn transition to diurnal chorus. The experience is the trajectory, not any single peak moment.
theory_nodes.description: Four-phase temporal development -- the night as a developing acoustic narrative.

theory_nodes.concept: E=9 temporal development calibration: not a simultaneous-maximum event. The full-night experience is temporally rich but calibrated below the simultaneous-peak E=10 entries because no single moment is at the ceiling of what the forest can produce. The E=9 is also the closing arc's resolution energy: after four E=20 (E=10+E=10) pairings, the arc resolves at E=18 (E=9+E=9). Temporal development paired with post-hardcore temporal development (TAAS).
theory_nodes.description: E=9 temporal development -- the calibration and the arc resolution.

theory_nodes.concept: Barred Owl / Northern Spotted Owl interaction: Barred Owl expansion (eastern NA origin, PNW expansion through 20th century) has created competitive exclusion pressure on the Spotted Owl. USFWS experimental removal programs initiated. The simultaneous occurrence of both species' calls in a winter owl chorus = old-growth forest with unresolved species interaction in progress. The acoustic event is an ecological document.
theory_nodes.description: Barred/Spotted interaction -- competitive exclusion, USFWS removal programs, acoustic documentation of ecological transition.

theory_nodes.concept: Northern Spotted Owl conservation significance: ESA threatened since 1990. Old-growth requirement drove Pacific Northwest timber wars late 1980s-1990s. The Spotted Owl is the species whose ESA listing had the greatest political and economic consequences in PNW history. Presence in the winter owl chorus = direct indicator of old-growth forest quality. The owl's call is the sound of protected old growth.
theory_nodes.description: Northern Spotted Owl -- ESA 1990, timber wars, old-growth indicator, political significance.

theory_nodes.concept: Frequency architecture of the winter owl chorus: Great Horned Owl 200-700 Hz (sub-bass and bass), Barred Owl 300-900 Hz (bass and lower midrange), Northern Spotted Owl 400-900 Hz (overlapping with Barred, distinct pattern), Western Screech-Owl 700-1,500 Hz (upper midrange, above hooters). The four species occupy different primary frequency ranges with some overlap, creating a frequency-organized acoustic community.
theory_nodes.description: Frequency architecture: four species occupying distinct primary frequency ranges in organized acoustic community.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. Temporal development pairing. TAAS songs develop through phases (intrasong variation). Owl chorus develops through the full winter night (four temporal phases). Both about what changes over time. First non-E=20 closing arc pairing. Arc resolves.
Both involve temporal development in acoustic events. 340 = dynamic event with temporal phases. 353 = full-night temporal development. Both at E-levels below the simultaneous-maximum events.
The Pileated Woodpecker is part of the same old-growth dense forest ecosystem that the winter owl chorus inhabits. Old-growth forest documentation across SPS entries.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. Temporal development pairing. TAAS: songs begin in one mode, arrive in another, trajectory is the experience. Owl chorus: dusk establishment, early night calling, deep night quiet, pre-dawn resurgence -- full night trajectory is the experience. Both about what changes over time rather than what peaks simultaneously. THE CLOSING ARC RESOLVES.

milestones: E=18 -- FIRST NON-E=20 CLOSING ARC PAIRING
closing_arc_complete: 349 Elk+Storm+Dawn E=10 → 350 Cascades Avalanche E=10 → 351 Insect+Bird+Rain E=10 → 352 River+Percussion+Wind E=10 → 353 Mixed Owl Chorus E=9. THE SPS CLOSING ARC COMPLETE.
